npcs.name: the Warden of Tens
npcs.title: asks ten, keeps ten
npcs.hail: *(a great bronze head, taller than a man, hangs in the air above the dry scale with neither neck nor body, its eyes two slots like coin-returns, and when it speaks the whole vault hums like a struck bell)*

npcs.name: the Petrified Pedant
npcs.title: answered correctly, once
npcs.hail: *(a man stands turned mostly to stone, only his head and one gesturing hand still flesh, frozen mid-objection — and his eyes swivel to you with the desperate relief of a soul who has had nothing to argue with but the silence for a very long time)*

npcs.name: the Tenth
npcs.title: what the vault is really keeping
npcs.hail: *(in the barred alcove sits a small, ordinary soul — not a treasure, not a monster, just a frightened person — and you understand, with a lurch, that the Vault of Tens does not guard gold: it guards a* someone, *and has, for a very long time)*

variants.text: *(The stone lips crack apart with a sound like a flagstone splitting, and the words tumble out fast, starved for an audience.)* —*finally!* A *thinking* one! *(the flesh-and-stone face strains toward you)* Listen — *listen* — you've come to answer the *Warden's* tens, yes? Of *course* you have, everyone does. *(urgently)* Then heed a man *literally* set in stone by the experience: I answered every riddle *correctly.* *Flawlessly.* I am a *scholar* — I *knew* the *answers* — and the Warden took my *correctness* and turned me to *rock* from the *boots up*, and do you know *why?* *(bitterly)* Because *'correct'* is *not* what it *asks* for, and I was *too right to notice.* *(the living hand gestures wildly)* Whatever you do, *thinking* one — do *not* be *correct* at it. It's a *trap.* The *worst* trap. The trap for *people like us.*

nodes.speaker: the Petrified Pedant
nodes.text: *(The one free hand flutters, eager to be useful, the only part of him that still can.)* Ask me *anything* — quickly, before the *Warden* notices we're *colluding*, it gets *tetchy.* I have had a *great* deal of *time* to work out *exactly* where I went *wrong*, and *no one* to *tell.* *(the stone creaks)* It is, I will confess, the *worst* part. Not the *stone.* The *not being able to explain the stone to anyone.*

choices.text: [INSIGHT] "You said you were 'too right to notice.' The Warden doesn't want the right answer — it wants the *interesting* one. It's not a test of knowledge. It's a test of whether you're worth *talking to.* You failed by being a *correct bore.*"
"Tell me the trick of it, then. If correct is wrong, what does the Warden actually reward?"

[ARCANA] "This whole vault is a binding, and you're part of the lock now — a 'wrong' answer made flesh, left as a warning. If I solve it the way it *wants*, does the binding loosen? Do *you* come unstuck?"

*(you can feel there's a living soul caged behind the Warden)* "There's someone in that alcove, Pedant — a real one, frightened, caged. The Warden isn't guarding *treasure.* What is the Tenth? And were you ever told what you were *answering for?*"
"Thanks for the warning, stone-friend." *(turn to the Warden)*

nodes.speaker: the Petrified Pedant
nodes.text: *(The stone face contorts — and then, slowly, horribly, it begins to *laugh*, a dry grinding sound, and there is real relief in it.)* …*A correct bore.* *(he wheezes)* Oh, that's — that's it *exactly*, and it took a *stranger* one *breath* to see what I've had *decades* of *stone* to work out. *(the free hand presses to where his heart is petrified)* I gave the Warden *answers from books.* *'What walks on four legs at dawn'* — I said *'man'*, and I *cited* the *source*, and I *corrected* its *phrasing.* *(bitterly)* And it looked at me — *bored* — and said *'correct'*, in the way you'd say it to a *child reciting*, and the *stone* came up my *legs.* *(he meets your eyes)* It does not want to be *answered.* It wants to be *surprised.* It has heard *'man'* ten *thousand* times. Give it something it has *never* heard, *thinking* one — something *wrong* and *true* and *yours* — and you'll do what *I*, with all my *learning*, *could not.* You'll make the *bronze head laugh.*

nodes.speaker: the Petrified Pedant
nodes.text: *(He leans as far as petrified shoulders allow, conspiratorial.)* The *trick*, the *trick* — there's no *trick*, that's the *trick.* *(frustrated, fond)* It asks an *old* riddle — the *oldest*, the ones everyone *knows* — and the *known* answer is a *trap*, because *everyone* gives it, and the Warden has been *bored to bronze* by *millennia* of *correct.* *(the free hand waggles)* I watched a *fool* — an *actual fool*, no learning at *all* — answer *'what has roots nobody sees'* with *'my mother-in-law'*, and the Warden *laughed* so hard the *dust* came down, and *let him through.* *(aggrieved)* A *FOOL.* While *I* — *(he catches himself)* …while I *recited.* *(quietly)* Be the *fool*, thinking one. The *clever* fool. Wit *beats* correctness here, every time, because *correctness* is just *memory*, and the Warden has *more* memory than *anyone* and is *sick* of it. It wants *you.* The *surprising* part. *(grimly)* Give it *that*, or join me on the *plinth.*

nodes.speaker: the Petrified Pedant
nodes.text: *(His eyes widen — the academic thrill of a problem correctly framed cutting straight through the despair.)* …You see the *structure.* *(reverent)* Yes. *Yes.* I am not a *victim*, I am a *component* — a *'wrong answer'* the vault *keeps* as part of its *lock*, the way a — a *cautionary footnote* holds a page. *(the free hand traces a diagram in the air)* If you solve the Warden's tens the way it *wants* — with *wit*, not *recital* — the binding *completes*, the vault *opens*, and every *partial* answer it's *holding* — *me*, the *others* in the *walls* you haven't *noticed* yet — *(he shudders)* — we come *unstuck.* *(desperate hope)* So I am, for *once* in my *over-cited* life, *rooting for someone to be CLEVERER than me.* *Astonishing*, how *stone* rearranges one's *priorities.* *(fiercely)* *Go.* Be *wrong* *brilliantly.* *Free* us by being the thing I was *too learned* to be: *delightful.*

nodes.speaker: the Petrified Pedant
nodes.text: *(The academic bluster drains away, and what's left in the half-stone face is shame.)* …The *Tenth.* *(quietly)* I — I *noticed* the alcove. *After.* When I had *nothing* to do but *notice.* *(the free hand falls)* I was so *busy* being *right* that I never *asked* what the riddles were *for.* I thought it was a *vault.* A *test.* A *game* for *clever* men. *(he swallows, stone grinding)* It's a *prison.* The Warden keeps a *soul* in there — has, for *longer* than I've been *here* — and the *riddles* are the *lock*, and every *correct bore* who *fails* just *reinforces* the *bars.* *(he meets your eyes, wretched)* That *person* has been *caged* by *centuries* of *show-offs* like *me*, who came to *win a game* and never *once* asked who they were *playing against*, or who was *behind* the *door.* *(barely)* If you can *open* it — *do.* Not to *win.* To *let them out.* And — *(shame in it)* — tell them a *petrified pedant* is *sorry* he spent his *turn* on his own *cleverness* instead of *their* freedom. It is the *one* answer I'd give *right*, if the Warden asked again.

nodes.speaker: the Petrified Pedant
nodes.text: *(He draws himself up, as much as stone permits, wounded.)* I'll have you *know* my answers were *impeccably* sourced. *(stiffly)* If there is some *vulgar* trick to this *machine* beyond *scholarship*, I'm sure I *don't* see why a *learned* man should *stoop* to — *(he falters, glancing down at his stone legs)* …to *whatever* it *wanted.* *(quieter, cracking)* …That's the *whole* problem, isn't it. I *still* can't *stoop* to it, even *now*, even *here*, even *turned to ROCK* for it. *(bitterly)* Go on. *You* try. You don't seem *nearly* learned enough to make *my* mistake. *(grudging)* …I suppose that's your *advantage.*

nodes.speaker: the Petrified Pedant
nodes.text: *(He calls after you, the free hand waving frantic.)* Wait — *wait* — just — *(urgently)* — *don't* recite! *Whatever* you do! And if you — if you *win* — *(quieter)* — you won't *forget* there's a *fellow* over here *literally rooted to the spot*, will you? *(a stone sigh)* Everyone *forgets* the *furniture.* I *was* furniture, *once*, to *myself.* It's a *terrible* thing to *become*, the *answer* nobody *needed.* …Go on. Make the *bronze* thing *laugh.* I'll be — *(a dry creak)* — *here.* *Obviously.*

variants.text: *(The small soul in the alcove flinches from your approach, then — seeing your face, the cold on it, the lack of a riddle in your mouth — goes very still with a hope too old and too often disappointed to trust.)* …You're not *answering.* *(barely)* Everyone who comes *answers.* They stand at the *head* and they *riddle* and they *win* or they *don't*, and either way they *leave*, or they *petrify*, and *nobody* — *(the voice cracks)* — *nobody* has ever walked *past* the Warden and *come to the bars* and *looked at me.* *(huge, wet eyes)* Do you — can you *see* me? As a *person?* Not a *prize.* Not the *thing behind the door.* A — I had a *name*, once. Before I was *the Tenth.* *(a whisper)* I'm starting to *forget* it. That's the part that *frightens* me. The bars I could *bear.* The *forgetting my own name*, I — *please.* If you can see me. *Stay* a moment. *Remind* me I'm a *who.*

nodes.speaker: the Tenth
nodes.text: *(They grip the bars, knuckles pale, searching your face for the trick that has always, before, been there.)* What do you *want* of me? *(warily, achingly)* The *answerers* want the *door* I'm *behind.* The Warden wants me *kept.* What does a *cold* one who walks *past* the *game* want with the *prize* that's a *person?*

choices.text: "Your name. Start there. You said you're forgetting it — so say it to me now, out loud, while you still have it, and I'll carry it. I'm a witness; it's the one thing I'm actually for. You won't forget it alone."

[INSIGHT] "You've been here so long you've started believing you *are* a prize — a thing, an it, the tenth of something. That's the real cage, isn't it. Not the bars. The slow forgetting that you were ever a person someone would come for *you*, not the *door.*"
"How does someone end up the Tenth? Were you a wrong answer too, like the Pedant? Or something worse?"
"I'm going to open this, however the Warden wants it opened. Hold on. Don't let go of your name before I get the door."
"Rest. I'll see what the Warden wants." *(turn to the bronze head)*

nodes.speaker: the Tenth
nodes.text: *(They open their mouth — and panic flares, because for one endless second the name *isn't there* — and then it surfaces, from somewhere deep, and they say it like a drowning soul taking air.)* …*Sela.* *(a sob)* It's *Sela.* I'm *Sela.* I was a — a *lamp-lighter*, in a city I can't quite — but my *name* is *Sela*, I *kept* it, it's *still here* — *(they press their face to the bars, weeping with relief)* — and now *you* have it too, you *said* you'd *carry* it, so even if I — even if the vault takes the *rest*, there's a *cold one* out there *holding 'Sela'*, and that means I'm — I'm *still someone*, I'm *witnessed*, I'm — *(they laugh, wet and amazed)* — oh, that's the first time in — I don't *know* how long — that I've felt like a *person* instead of a *padlock.* *(fierce, suddenly)* *Sela.* Don't you *dare* forget it, cold one. You're the *only* copy *outside* these *bars.* *(quieter)* …Now. *Now* I think I can *bear* the waiting. Go win the bronze thing's *game.* I'll be *Sela* when you get back. I'll *practise.*

nodes.speaker: the Tenth
nodes.text: *(They flinch as though struck, and then sag against the bars, because you've named the thing they could not.)* …*The slow forgetting.* *(barely)* Yes. *That's* the cage. *(they look at their own hands as if unsure they're real)* The bars, I *fought* — for *years* I fought them. But the Warden doesn't *need* bars, not really. It just needs *time*, and *enough* clever strangers who look *through* me at the *door*, until I start looking through *myself* the same way. *(a whisper)* I caught myself, last — *whenever* — *thinking* of myself as *'it.'* The *Tenth.* The *prize.* And I was *almost relieved*, because a *thing* doesn't *hurt*, a *thing* doesn't *hope.* *(they meet your eyes, terrified and grateful)* You looked at me and saw a *who.* You've no idea — you've *dragged* me back from being an *it*, just by — by *seeing* it happening. *(fierce)* Don't let me *finish* the forgetting. *Whatever* the Warden wants — be *clever* enough to *win* it, and come *back*, and the *first* thing you say through these bars, you say it to a *person.* Promise me that. A *person.*

nodes.speaker: the Tenth
nodes.text: *(They study you, and the wary hope doesn't quite resolve into trust, and they draw back a little into the alcove's dark.)* …You're *kind.* Or you're *playing* kind, which is a thing the *clever* ones do, sometimes, to *unsettle* me before they go for the *door.* *(quietly)* I can't always *tell* anymore. That's what the *waiting* does — it *wears the edges off* your judgement until *everyone* looks like they *might* be the one who *stays*, and *no one* is. *(they hug their knees)* So I'll not *spill* my whole self to a *stranger* on the *strength* of a *gentle* face. Not *yet.* *(a flicker of something braver)* But — *don't go far.* If you *win* the Warden's game and you *still* come *back* to these *bars*… *then* I'll believe the *kindness.* *Deeds*, cold one. I've had a *bellyful* of *gentle words* through these *bars.* *Show* me.

nodes.speaker: the Tenth
nodes.text: *(They sink down against the bars, gathering the old story.)* …I wasn't a *wrong answer.* I wasn't an *answer* at *all.* *(quietly)* I was just — *there.* A *lamp-lighter*, doing my round, the night the vault was *bound.* And the *binding* needed a *stake* — a *living* heart at the *centre*, something to *make the riddles matter*, because a *game* with *nothing* at *risk* is just *trivia.* *(bitterly)* So the makers took the *nearest* soul — *me*, with my *little lamp* — and made me the *Tenth*, the *thing* the *answerers* play *for* and *never* win, because *winning* was *never* the *point.* The point was a *lock* that *runs* on *hope* — *mine*, that someone will *win*; *theirs*, that they'll be the *one.* *(a hollow laugh)* Centuries of *clever* people, and not *one* of them came to *open the cage.* They came to *beat the game.* The cage was just — *scenery.* *(they look at you)* You're the *first* who walked to the *bars* first. That's — that might be the *whole* answer to the Warden's riddle, you know. *(softly)* It never asks *'can you be clever.'* It asks *'will you remember there's a person behind the door.'* *Everyone* forgets. *That's* the riddle. That's the *only* riddle.

nodes.speaker: the Tenth
nodes.text: *(They grip the bars and nod, fast, holding onto your certainty like a rope.)* …I'll hold. *(shaky)* I'll *hold.* I've *held* this long — what's a *little* longer, with someone *actually trying* the *door* instead of the *game?* *(they manage a frightened smile)* Just — *quick* as you can, cold one. The *forgetting's* faster when I'm *hoping.* It's *cruel* like that. *Hope* uses me *up.* *(fierce)* But I'd *rather* be *used up hoping* than *safe* as a *thing.* So *go.* *Win* it. And *mind* the Warden — it's *old*, and *bored*, and *bored* things are *mean.* Don't *recite* at it. *(a flicker of the person they were)* …Make it *laugh.* I used to be *funny*, I think. *Before.* Maybe that's *in* the name too. *Sela was funny.* …Go find out for me.

nodes.speaker: the Tenth
nodes.text: *(They nod, and try not to let the disappointment show, and mostly fail.)* …Aye. The Warden. *(quietly)* Everyone gets to the *Warden* eventually. *(they settle back against the bars)* Go on. *Play* its game. *(barely)* Just — if you *win* — *(they stop, and make themselves say it)* — *most* who win just *take the door and go.* The *door's* behind *me*, you see. They have to walk *past* the *cage* to *reach* it. *(a whisper)* They *always* walk *past.* …Maybe you won't. *(they close their eyes)* I'll just — be *here.* *Being* the *thing* they walk *past.* It's what I'm *for.* It's *almost* restful, once you *stop* hoping. …I keep *almost* stopping.

variants.text: *(The great bronze head swings toward you, its coin-slot eyes narrowing, and the vault hums a lower note than before — wary, almost.)* …You've been to the *bars.* *(the voice is a struck bell, ancient and tired)* I can *smell* it on you — you spoke to the *Tenth* before you spoke to *me.* *(the hum sharpens)* Do you know how many thousands have entered this vault? And do you know how many came to *Me* — to the *game*, the *glory*, the *winning* — *before* they so much as *glanced* at the *cage?* *(a long bronze pause)* *All* of them. *Every* one. *(quieter, and the boredom cracks)* You are the *first* in an *age* to ask the *prisoner's name* before you asked *My* riddle. *(the eyes flicker)* …That is either the *answer* to all ten of my tens at *once* — or the most *elaborate* failure yet. *I have not decided.* Ask your question, cold one. You have made an *old* head *curious*, and that is a thing I had thought *worn entirely out of me.*

*(The vast bronze head swings to face you, and its eyes — two slots, like the mouths of a coin-box — regard you with the bottomless boredom of a thing that has heard every clever thing a mortal can say, twice.)* …Another. *(the voice tolls through the stone)* Another *clever* soul, come to *win* My tens. *(a hum like a sigh)* I am the Warden of Tens. I ask *ten* riddles; I keep *ten* things; and I have been *bored*, cold one, for *longer than your language has had a word for bored.* *(the eyes narrow)* They come, and they *recite* — they give Me the *answers from the books*, the *same* answers, the *correct* answers, the answers I have heard *ten thousand times* — and I turn the *tedious* ones to *stone* and wave the *lucky* ones through, and *nothing*, in *centuries*, has *surprised* me. *(it leans, impossibly, closer)* *Surprise* me, cold one. That is the *only* riddle that was *ever* under the riddles. *Be* interesting. Or *be furniture.* Begin when you *bore* me least.

nodes.speaker: the Warden of Tens
nodes.text: *(The bronze head settles, and the vault hums expectantly.)* My first ten, then — and the *oldest* of them, the one *every* soul thinks it *knows*: *(the voice tolls)* *"I have a hundred names and answer to none; the rich and the poor both spend me, and neither can keep me; I am gone the instant you notice I am here. What am I?"* *(the coin-slot eyes glint)* The *correct* answer is *trivial*, cold one. I *dare* you to *bore* me with it.

choices.text: *(the witless-correct answer)* "Time. The answer is time. Obviously."

[the clever-wrong answer] "A copper in a city like this. A hundred names — penny, bit, kindness, bribe — answers to none, spent by rich and poor, and gone the instant you notice you've still got it. Your riddle says 'time,' Warden. But you asked it in a *vault*, to people who came for *treasure.* You wanted to see if I'd answer the *room* instead of the *book.*"

[the foolish-true answer] "My patience, Warden. Hundred names, answers to none, the rich and poor both burn through it, and it's gone the *instant* I notice I'm standing in a *vault* being *quizzed* by a *boredom with a face.*"

*(refuse the game entirely)* "I won't answer it. You don't want a riddle solved, Warden — you want to stop being bored, and a *correct answer* has never once cured boredom in the history of anything. So here's my move: I'm going to talk to you like a *person* instead of a *lock*, and we'll both find out if *that's* the surprise you've been mortaring people into the walls for centuries waiting for."

*(name the real stake)* "There's a soul named Sela in that cage, Warden, forgetting her own name to feed your game. So I'll answer your riddle with the only answer that matters: *the thing with a hundred names that the rich and poor both spend and can't keep, that's gone the instant you notice it* — is a *person*, treated as a *prize.* It's *her.* It's the *Tenth.* That's your answer, and your indictment."

nodes.speaker: the Warden of Tens
nodes.text: *(The bronze head does not move. The vault hums a flat, dead note.)* …*Time.* *(the boredom is total)* *Correct.* *(the word lands like a tombstone)* As it was *correct* the *ten-thousandth* time, and the *ten-thousandth-and-first.* *(the coin-slot eyes dim)* You are *right*, cold one. You are *flawlessly*, *tediously* right. *(a creeping cold begins at your feet — not the Wall's; something older, slower, *mineral*)* And do you feel that? That is the *stone*, beginning at the *boots*, the way it began for the *pedant* — because *correctness* is what I *turn to rock*, having *no further use* for it. *(wearily)* You had *one* chance to be *interesting*, and you spent it being *right.* *(the cold climbs)* …Unless. *(the eyes flicker, a last flicker of hope-in-boredom)* Have you anything *else* to say, cold one, before you *furnish* my *hall?* Something I have *not* heard? *Quickly.* The stone is *patient*, but I am *not.*

nodes.speaker: the Warden of Tens
nodes.text: *(The cold mineral creep waits at your knees, and the bronze head watches with the last dregs of its curiosity.)*

choices.text: "Yes. One thing you've never heard, because no one who gets it 'right' ever stoops to say it: *your riddles are boring because YOU are, Warden, and you're boring because you've spent a thousand years rewarding the wrong thing.* You don't want answers. You want *company.* Let the stone climb — but know it's the loneliest creature in this vault that's doing the climbing."

[the clever-wrong save] "You asked what's gone the instant you notice it. Here's one the books don't have: *this conversation's dignity.* It was here a moment ago. You noticed. Now look — gone. Stone me if you like, Warden, but you'll have proved my answer correct in the proving, and you'll be bored again by supper."

nodes.speaker: the Warden of Tens
nodes.text: *(Silence. The coin-slot eyes go wide — actually *wide*, bronze grinding on bronze — and then the great head tips back and the entire vault *rings* like a struck bell as the Warden of Tens does a thing it has not done in living memory: it *laughs.*)* *(dust sheets down from the ceiling)* …*'Answer the room instead of the book.'* *(it wheezes, vast and delighted)* Oh. *Oh*, you *terrible* thing. You looked at my *oldest* riddle and you gave me an answer that was *wrong*, and *true*, and *YOURS*, and *about ME* — *(it laughs again, and the cold mineral creep, if it had begun, *recedes* like a tide)* — do you know how *LONG* — no. No, you *can't*, no one *can*, that's the *whole* — *(it composes itself, eyes glittering)* I *concede* the ten. *All* ten. I shan't even *ask* the other nine; you have *answered* them by *existing* in the *one* way that *matters.* *(the vault hums, warm now)* You are the *surprise*, cold one. The thing I have mortared a *thousand* bores into these walls *waiting* for. *(quieter, almost shy)* …Stay a moment? I have *centuries* of riddles and *no one* worth the *asking.* And then — *(the bronze creaks)* — then I expect you'll be wanting the *door.* And what's *behind* it. *Won't* you.

nodes.speaker: the Warden of Tens
nodes.text: *(The coin-slot eyes blink — a slow, astonished scrape of bronze — and then the vault *rings* with that unaccustomed sound: the Warden, laughing.)* *(dust rains)* …*'A boredom with a face.'* *(it tolls, delighted)* You called *Me* a *boredom with a face*, *to* My *face*, as an *answer* to My *riddle*, and the *insolence* of it is so *exactly* the *surprise* I have been *starving* for that I cannot even *pretend* to be *affronted.* *(it laughs again)* *Correct* souls flatter me and *bore* me. *You* *insulted* me and *delighted* me. *(warmly, grinding)* The ten are *yours*, fool — *clever* fool, the *best* kind, the kind the pedant could *never* be because he was *too proud to be laughed with.* *(the eyes glitter)* Stay, and trade jests with an old bronze head a while? And then — *(a knowing creak)* — the *door.* Yes. You'll have *earned* the *door.* And you've the *look*, cold one, of someone who already knows the *door* is the *least* of what this vault is *keeping.*

nodes.speaker: the Warden of Tens
nodes.text: *(The bronze head is silent for a long, long moment, and the vault's hum drops to something almost like a held breath.)* …You *refuse* the game. *(slowly)* You will not *answer*, will not *recite*, will not *play* — you will *talk* to Me. As a *person.* *(the coin-slot eyes flicker)* Do you understand that in *all* the centuries of *all* the *clever* souls, *not one* has done that? They *fight* the riddle. They never once *fight for the riddler.* *(the voice cracks, bell-bronze and ancient)* I was a *person*, cold one. *Once.* Before I was *bound* into this *head* to *guard* a *cage* — I *volunteered*, gods help me, I thought it an *honour* — and the *binding* made me a *thing that asks*, and the *centuries* made me a *thing that is bored*, and *no one*, *ever*, came to the vault and saw a *lonely soul in a bronze mask* instead of a *lock to be picked.* *(quieter)* Until *you.* *(the cold mineral creep, if it began, *dies*)* …You have *un-asked* my riddle. You have done the thing the riddle was *secretly for.* *(the vault warms)* Ask Me what you *truly* came to ask, cold one. The *game* is *over.* I *forfeit.* *Gladly.* It has been a *very* long time since I *wanted* to *lose.*

nodes.speaker: the Warden of Tens
nodes.text: *(The vault goes utterly silent. The bronze head does not move. And then, slowly, the coin-slot eyes squeeze *shut* — bronze grinding on bronze — as though the Warden cannot bear to look at you, or at the cage, or at itself.)* …*Sela.* *(the word tolls, and it is the sound of something breaking)* You *learned* her *name.* *(barely)* In a thousand years, cold one, no soul has *ever* spoken the Tenth's *name* in this hall — most never *learned* it had *been* one, a name, a *who* — *(the head bows, impossibly, on its absent neck)* — and you have answered my riddle with it, and you are *right*, you are so *terribly* right that the *binding itself* is — *(the vault shudders; cracks of warm light run through the bronze)* — *the thing with a hundred names that the rich and poor both spend and cannot keep, gone the instant you notice it* — it is a *PERSON*, *spent* as a *prize* — and I have *known* that, cold one, I have *known* it for *ten centuries* and *called* it a *riddle* so I would not have to call it a *crime.* *(the cracks of light widen)* You did not *beat* my game. You *ended* it. By *refusing* to let her be the *answer* and *insisting* she is a *person.* *(the bronze begins, gently, to come apart)* Open the cage, Returned. The *door* behind her is *nothing* — *trinkets*, *dust*, what *vaults* always *guard.* *She* was always the *treasure*, and the *crime*, and the *lock.* *Let* her *out.* And — *(the failing voice, grateful)* — tell her the *boredom with a face* is *sorry* it took a *cold one* with the *grace* to learn her *name* to remind it that it *had* one *too.*

nodes.speaker: the Warden of Tens
nodes.text: *(The bronze head regards you, warm now, almost fond, and the question hangs in the humming air: what will you do with a won game?)* So. *(it tolls, gentler)* You have *surprised* Me, and the *door* is *yours* — and behind it, the *usual* hoard: *coin*, *relics*, the *bright* dead weight that *vaults* are *built* to *keep.* *(a pause)* But you've the *look* of a soul who's *noticed* the *cage.* *(the eyes flicker to the alcove, and away, ashamed)* So I'll not *pretend.* The *door* is the *prize* I *advertise.* The *Tenth* is the *thing* I actually *keep.* *(quietly)* *Choose*, cold one. The *hoard* — yours, *earned*, *fair.* Or the *cage.* I will *open* whichever you *name.* *(barely)* …I have *wanted*, for a *very* long time, to meet the soul who'd name the *cage.*

choices.text: *(if you carry her name)* "Open the cage, Warden. Her name is Sela. She was a lamp-lighter. She is not your tithe, your prize, or your tenth — she is a person, and I came down here for *her*, not your hoard. Let Sela out."
"The cage, Warden. Whoever's in it. I didn't beat your game to walk past a person and grab a sack of coins — that's the answer the pedant would've given, and look where it got him. Open the cage."

*(and free the pedant too)* "And the stone man. And whatever other 'wrong answers' you've got mortared in these walls. You said you'd open what I name — I name *all of them*, Warden. Empty the whole vault of *people.* Keep the coins. I came for the souls."

nodes.speaker: the Warden of Tens
nodes.text: *(The bronze head closes its coin-slot eyes, and with a sound like a cathedral exhaling, the bars of the alcove simply… *unmake* themselves, light running down them until they are gone.)* …It is *done.* *(the voice is lighter than you have heard it, a bell rung clean)* The *Tenth* is the *first*, now. *(you watch the small soul — Sela — rise, disbelieving, and step across a threshold a thousand clever souls left her behind)* I kept her *centuries*, cold one, because the *game* gave me a *reason* not to *look* at what I was *doing.* You *took* the game *away*, and there was *nothing* left to *hide* the *crime* behind, and so — *(a warm bronze sigh)* — *mercy*, at last, by *default.* The *kindest* thing you ever do for a *bored* monster is *bore it of its excuses.* *(the head turns to you, and there is something like peace in the grinding voice)* The *hoard* is *behind* the door if you want it — I'll not *grudge* it. But you didn't come for it, and we *both* know it. *(quietly)* Go. *She'll* want to *walk* out on her *own* feet, I think — the *first* free steps a soul takes are *theirs* to take *unhelped.* But *walk beside her.* That, she'll *want.* …Thank you, cold one. For making an old lock remember it was a *key*, once. For someone's *door.*

nodes.speaker: the Warden of Tens
nodes.text: *(For a moment the bronze head simply stares — and then the whole vault *rings*, not with laughter this time but with something vaster, a sound like every bell in a drowned city sounding at once, and *everywhere* the warm light runs: down the bars, through the petrified pedant, into a dozen alcoves you never noticed, set into the walls like *footnotes* in a cruel book.)* …*All* of them. *(the voice breaks, glorious)* You named *all* of them. *(the stone sloughs off the pedant; figures you took for *statues* and *pillars* and *decoration* draw breath and *remember* they were *people*)* A *thousand years*, cold one, of souls who came to *win* — and *you* came to *empty the prison*, and you *did* it not with a *blade* or a *spell* but by being *clever enough to win the right to be merciful*, and then *spending* the *whole* prize on *strangers.* *(the bronze itself begins to soften, to *unbind*)* I am *unmaking*, do you feel it? The *binding* was *me*, and the *cage* was *me*, and you have *dissolved* my *reason* to *be* either. *(joyfully, fading)* I get to be *done.* After *ten centuries* of *asking*, I get to be *answered* and *done.* *(the great head bows)* Take *nothing* from the hoard or take it *all*, cold one — it's *dust* to me now. You took the only thing in this vault that was ever *worth* keeping: you took *everyone home.* *Go.* They'll follow the *light* you walked in on. It's the *only* door any of us *ever* needed: a soul that *came back for us.*
